author: @Wes_CWW
text: I use OpenSCAD and love it because it is FOSS and although it is code driven, it is not difficult to learn.

author: @DannyThompson54
text:
I guess it all depend on what it is you are mainly engaged in designing and printing. But I am 3D printing since November 2013, mostly technical and practical stuff, parts, even mini-Drones back in the day!  I don't recall when TinkerCAD came into my life but I am using it to this very day with what I can only describe as complete success. I have actually had zero need to use anything else, although have kicked the tyres of Fusion 360 and FreeCAD for example.   I have a few times had to re-work other people's designs, but generally am happier designing from scratch. I always advise TinkerCAD as somewhere for those new to 3D printing go to.  There is nothing more frustrating than failures, it'll soon put folk of designing their own.  TinkerCAD really helps to achieve early success. 

Thanks for the video, it was really entertaining. I've bookmarked it to share to the next person who asks me about 3D printing 🙂

author: @markusgarvey
text:
I bought a Snapmaker printer over 2 years ago and have not used yet. I tried to get the filament to load , it wouldn't  and I had more important stuff to do. I have been Network admin, hacker, computer builder, repaired motherboards, so I know I can figure it out, it's all about the time i need to  spend doing it. Im 61 so time has become a premium. I was a bit intimidated by the software since I have never worked with autocad except to get the program to run for someone else. 

Now I'm working with an old carburetor for a wheel loader. It's the old updraft type and there is a plastic venturi in it that is broken. 

So I have the printer on my desk and broken piece i need to make is sitting in the printer. So I guess that is greeting closer! It has to be precise to fit and work correctly. I am gonna mess around with the cad program you mentioned. I will have to create it from scratch, because I know there are none that were done already. 

You have inspired me once again to try something. My printer will only make small pieces, and it's a bit obsolete. It is big enough to make the piece I need, so here we go. Thanks again Robert!

author: @Enjoymentboy
text:
This is fantastic advice and it is exactly what I have been doing myself.  I first started printing pre-designed items from files I found online and then played with my printer/slicer settings to see how it affected the print.  The way I use my 3D printer is purely utilitarian (ie functional parts and nothing decorative) so CAD programs only and nothing for 3D design.  I've settled on FreeCAD as I prefer a stand-alone solution instead of something through an online portal.  Basically, I want to be able to work without having to rely on an internet connection.  The single biggest challenge I've encountered is in trying to understand the logic of WHY a utility does something the way that it does as opposed to HOW it does it.  Most of the tutorials and lessons I've watched focus on "This is what this function does and how you use it" but this is not how my brain works.  I need to learn by having a specific task in mind and learning what I need to do in order to accomplish it.  Luckily there are a few really good youtubers who have small videos that do exactly that for using FreeCAD.

Having said all of this, after watching this video I think I will give TinkerCAD another go.  The way you showed things here absolutely fit in with how I learn...so thank you for this.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's how I learn too mate - by doing

replies.author: @DannyThompson54
replies.text: Oooh .... Oooh ... *waves* ... Me too! There are very many of us! Learn by doing.  Been using TinkerCAD for as long as I can remember it being available. I promote it to every who asks what to use to design stuff.

author: @grendel1960a
text:
I have rarely felt the need to go to thingiverse for a part, generally I will think it through and design a part for myself. but (and its a huge but) when I started 3d printing I was anything but your typical 3d printer person, I knew CAD, but I knew nothing about the printers, so I approached the hobby from the  angle that i could draw any part I could visualise. then i just let the printer do its thing. compared to when I got my first printer, nowadays it is so much easier, you really do just send the file to the printer and let it get on with it now.
I see where Rob is coming from though, you do need to have a go and get a few prints under your belt before trying to alter or draw your own part, 
and the other advice is take small steps, dont try and draw something too complex straight away, maybe just draw a part of it, next time draw a different part, then join them together, each time printing the result - filament is relatively cheap, working up to the more complex part over time. 
I have drawn up in CAD a 3d model of a friends boat, then built it from wood and glue, the CAD drawing took me (a trained professional CAD operator) nearly 3 years as I slowly added detail (its taken me 5 years so far on the actual model) but anything can be done in small enough bites, and as I have built it I have learned, I have made 3d printed parts, I have used CNC engraving to make parts, I have learned to machine tiny parts from brass, I even made a 1/12 scale chair with spindle turned legs- all skills I didnt have when I started (but that I do have now). 
All you really need to do is to start, and do a little bit every day, and you will learn, you will get better, and one day you will gaze upon your creation and realise all the new skill you have learned in the process, and be happy with your creation, whether it be a 3d cube or a model boat.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate and nicely put

author: @danyulMark
text:
Hey Robert! Been watching your channel - your passion and excitement for these DIY projects is contagious! : )

I have a idea and wanted to know if you or any one in the comments thinks this is possible/worth.

DYNAMO GENERATOR to capacitor/s coupled with a SPLIT battery pack, using magnets placed N/S on the outside of electric motor and a copper coil placed on both sides of the fork.
When the pack drains to halfway or however you split it (60-40, 70-30)
the dynamo turns on charging one half of the pack with the energy created by the second side. the dynamo would create a little resistance but at least you could charge the dead half of the battery to maybe 30-40% or more, ?? Wouldn't last forever but maybe could prolong the range/power?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the capacitor idea is good mate - it matches the output of the dynamo to the input of the battery - it basically corrects for the difference in power density of the two devices. I don't see the point of using the battery to run the dynamo to charge the other battery though - as dynamos are 60-85% efficient all you are actually going to do is increase self discharge of the whole store - or at least that's how it seems to me

replies.author: @danyulMark
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Sorry forgot to mention that I would be installing this on an electric bicycle as a way to recapture some of the spent energy.

author: @ToninFightsEntropy
text:
Thanks for this, I might give Tinker CAD a go!
Lol I've been meaning to switch to a CAD package for 3D modelling for about 5 years now 😅
That's what 24 years of Polygon modelling does to ya (CAD is Curves/NURBS, which is so weirdly different despite some conversion possibilities and that printers actually need Polygons lol (STL is compressed to triangulated polygons with no edge-flow), but Surfaces make a billion times more sense for CAD stuff, especially with thicknesses of objects, just for the changeability). Just very hard to get out of the habit of using comfortable software (though Maya is definitely the wrong tool for the job long-term).
The power of being able to model for your own prints though is phenomenal.. when I have a problem it's my first go-to. I almost never print other people's models.. too buggy for my liking 😂but also when you need unique fixtures or just want to test something, the things you can do with modelling skills and a printer are ridiculous. 3D modelling is definitely a worthwhile skill to have! Just do more tutorials when you get stuck, and the tools are so much easier to learn now, and hopefully the methods, too. That object subtraction (Boolean), for example.. that's top notch mate! And curves handle that so much better than polygons lol

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: try tinkercad, if that doesnt work for you theres plenty of other pieces of software, just try them until you find one that works for you, above all as Rob says, have fun. I have been a CAD draughtsman for 40 years, and I still have fun every day (its what keeps me going and stops me retiring - well that and my work colleagues) basically its just a case of finding the software that makes the conversion from what you know to 3d work for you.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

replies.author: @ToninFightsEntropy
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering One of the key lessons I have to give when it comes to 3D modelling (as a former teacher in this field), is that you don't learn separately how to make a chair, a table, a kettle, a car, or even an elephant, but instead you learn the tools with which you can make all of those things and many more.
(I say this because on their first assignment to model a chair, at the more relaxed college I worked where it was rare to be able to lecture a whole room, I'd often find people googling "how to model a chair", but I'd never see anybody googling "polygonal topology", "edge flow control", or even just "how to extrude shapes" or even the even simpler "basic modelling tools in [software]"!)

But I think it's funny because the same applies in real life.. you don't learn how to make one wardrobe, you learn how to saw, drill, screw, and tac.
Tools may be used in different proportions between projects, but it's the tools you learn if you really want to get a lot done without just copying what other people did ;)
(I can picture you saying this in one of your motivational videos, Rob, so I read it in your voice while I was writing the stuff outside of brackets lol 🤣feel free to quote me!)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @ToninFightsEntropy indeed mate - to be honest when it comes to pedagogy I try to be flexible - I must admit I highlight kinaesthetic methods as I personally feel they are underrepresented - but really learning is most effective when learning style and teaching style are matched. It's a question of here's a job what tools do I need or here's a tool what jobs can it do - some folks learn best with one approach, some learn best with the other - unfortunately there is never one solution lol

replies.author: @ToninFightsEntropy
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Absolutely! Another trick I learned that was helpful for some, was to set them up to teach what they're doing, to someone else, because by trying to explain something people think they don't understand, they often find they have the knowledge to fill the gaps and then realise they had it all along even if it didn't always make sense to them. It might not work for everybody, and the better students could definitely benefit more from it the easiest, but it was also a good way to get people out of a rut when they didn't think they knew what they were doing, I'd ask them to try to explain the task to someone else, as best they could, and you'd see them figure it out in about 10 seconds a lot of the time 🤣`
